Palace]][[en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]][[fa:قصر اومی اوتسو]][[ja:近江大津宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]][[en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]][[fa:قصر اومی اوتسو]][[ja:近江大津宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11638487]] Wikidata name: Ōmi Ōtsu Palace [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site | name =Ōmi Ōtsu Palace | native_name =近江大津宮 | native_name_lang = | image = Omi Otsu-no-miya, dairi-seiden-ato.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Ōmi Ōtsu Palace ruins | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ōmi Ōtsu Palace | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|1|41.9|N|135|51|18.0|E|region:JP-25|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu, Shiga]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} | type = palace ruins | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 7th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}} | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no public facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Ōmi Ōtsu Palace'''|近江大津宮| Ōmi Ōtsu-no-miya}} was an imperial palace built by [[:en:Emperor Tenchi|Emperor Tenchi]] in {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}} Japan in what is now the city of {{ill|Ōtsu|en|Ōtsu|WD=Q202907|lt=Ōtsu}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture}}, Japan. It served as the capital of Japan for a five-year period from 667 to 672 AD. Also known as the {{nihongo|'''Ōtsu Palace'''|大津宮| Ōtsu-no-miya}}, {{nihongo|'''Shiga no Miyako'''|志賀の都||}}, it was most frequently referred to in ancient sources as the {{nihongo|'''Ōmi Ōtsu-no-miya'''|水海大津宮||}}.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> It was at this location that the {{ill|Ōmi Code|en|Ōmi Code|WD=Q526944|lt=Ōmi Code}} and the family registry system were promulgated, which laid the foundations for the later ''[[ritsuryō]]'' state. It location was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1979, with the area under protection expanded in 2007<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/163041|title=近江大津宮錦織遺跡|trans-title= Ōmi Ōtsu PalaceNishigori Site|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref>
==History== In 660 AD, during the reign of [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]], the Korean kingdom of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] fell to an alliance of {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China}}. Baekje was a close ally of ancient Japan, and viewed as a bulwark against possible Silla or Tang expansion. Crown Prince Naka no Ōe, later to become Emperor Tenji, and Empress Saimei decided to dispatch an expeditionary force to restore the Baekje kingdom, but this was decisively defeated by the Silla-Tang alliance at the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|en|Battle of Baekgang|WD=Q608727|lt=Battle of Baekgang}} in 663 AD. This ended Japanese involvement in the Korean Peninsula for centuries, and fearing that Japan would be subject to a counter-invasion by Silla-Tang armies, a large number of mountain-top castles were constructed across western Japan. It was with this background that Emperor Tenchi decided to relocate the capital from {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka}} to the shore of {{ill|Lake Biwa|en|Lake Biwa|WD=Q200239|lt=Lake Biwa}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province}}. The reason for the transfer is not entirely clear. It is speculated that the location of the new capital on the shores of Lake Biwa facilitated communications and transportation to the {{ill|Sea of Japan|en|Sea of Japan|WD=Q27092|lt=Sea of Japan}} coast, and the location was on the major ancient {{ill|Tōsandō|en|Tōsandō|WD=Q1334011|lt=Tōsandō}} and {{ill|Hokurikudō|en|Hokurikudō|WD=Q3139239|lt=Hokurikudō}} highways. Another possible reason was that the emperor felt the need to relocate from the entrenched political factions and interests in Asuka in order to have a freer hand at the radical reforms necessary to create a new political system toEICcounter the threats from abroad. In any case, per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', there was great dissatisfaction in Asuka regarding the move, which led to major riots and arson.
Emperor Tenchi died in 671. Emperor Tenchi had originally designated his brother, [[:en:Prince Ōama|Prince Ōama]], as his successor, but later changed his mind in favor of his son, {{ill|Emperor Kōbun|en|Emperor Kōbun|WD=Q357242|lt=Prince Ōtomo}}. This led to the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War}}, during which Prince Ōtomo killed himself after reigning for less than a year, and the Ōtsu Palace was burned down. Prince Ōama succeeded to the throne as the {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|WD=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu}}, and moved the capital back to Asuka.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
In the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, the capital was moved to [[Heijō-kyō]], and the {{ill|Ōmi Kokuchō|en|Ōmi Kokuchō|WD=Q11638479|lt=provincial capital of Ōmi Province}} was built at Seta (now a neighborhood of the city of Ōtsu). The site of ruins of the Ōtsu Palace was named {{nihongo|Furutsu|古津||}}, meaning "old port", but the name was restored to "Ōtsu" by order of [[Emperor Kanmu]]. However, over time the exact location of the palace became lost in later centuries, and especially with growing nationalism in the late {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} and {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō era}}, there was strong desire to relocate it. Theories were advanced whenever ruins were found in the approximate location, which included the sites of {{ill|Sūfuku-ji (Ōtsu)|en|Sūfuku-ji (Ōtsu)|WD=Q11477101|lt=Sūfuku-ji}} and the {{ill|Minamishigachō temple ruins|en|Minamishigachō temple ruins|WD=Q109239206|lt=Minamishigachō temple ruins}} amongst many other possible candidates. As the palace existed for only five years and did not make use of [[:en:Roof tile|Roof tile]]s, very little physical evidence survived. In 1974, during {{ill|Rescue archaeology|en|Rescue archaeology|WD=Q653838|lt=Rescue archaeology}} on the site of a new residential area in the Nishikiori neighborhood of Ōtsu, 13 pillar holes which are thought to have been the remnants of a monumental south-facing gate were discovered. Further [[archaeological excavation]]s have found {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue}} and {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}} pottery, the presence of a double corridor extending from the South Gate to a large main hall, and the remnants of moats and earthen ramparts. The layout of structures were similar to that of the {{ill|Naniwa-kyō|en|Naniwa-kyō|WD=Q1042338|lt=Naniwa Palace}} which had been discovered in Osaka and which was nearly contemporary with the Ōtsu Palace. The total area of the Ōtsu Palace site is estimated to be 700 meters north-to-south by 400 meters east-to-west.EICThe site has been preserved as an [[:en:Archaeological park|Archaeological park]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The ruins of the Ōmi Ōtsu Palace are located in the western part of modern city of Ōtsu, about 500 meters north of {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company}} [[:en:Nishi-Ōtsu Station|Nishi-Ōtsu Station]] or a two-minute walk from {{ill|Ōmijingūmae Station|en|Ōmijingūmae Station|WD=Q8081616|lt=Ōmijingūmae Station}} on the {{ill|Keihan Electric Railway|en|Keihan Electric Railway|WD=Q1188274|lt=Keihan Electric Railway}} [[:en:Ishiyama Sakamoto Line|Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shiga)]]
==References== *{{cite book | last = Whitney Hall | first = John |author2=Delmer M Brown |author3=Kozo Yamamura | title = The Cambridge History of Japan | date = 1988 | pages = 42–44 | publisher = Cambridge University Press | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=nCJwEDzyxNgC&dq=Shigaraki+miya&pg=PA42 | isbn = 0-521-22352-0 }}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace}} *[https://www.biwako-visitors.jp/spot/detail/971/Shiga-Biwako Tourist Information] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.rekihaku.otsu.shiga.jp/event/jyousetsu/theme_6.html Otsu City Museum of History] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://oumijingu.org/pages/112/ Omi Jingu homepage] {{in lang|ja}}

Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture}}, Japan. It served as the capital of Japan for a five-year period from 667 to 672 AD. Also known as the {{nihongo|'''Ōtsu Palace'''|大津宮| Ōtsu-no-miya}}, {{nihongo|'''Shiga no Miyako'''|志賀の都||}}, it was most frequently referred to in ancient sources as the {{nihongo|'''Ōmi Ōtsu-no-miya'''|水海大津宮||}}.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> It was at this location that the {{ill|Ōmi Code|en|Ōmi Code|WD=Q526944|lt=Ōmi Code}} and the family registry system were promulgated, which laid the foundations for the later ''[[ritsuryō]]'' state. It location was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1979, with the area under protection expanded in 2007<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/163041|title=近江大津宮錦織遺跡|trans-title= Ōmi Ōtsu PalaceNishigori Site|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref>
==History== In 660 AD, during the reign of [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]], the Korean kingdom of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] fell to an alliance of {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China}}. Baekje was a close ally of ancient Japan, and viewed as a bulwark against possible Silla or Tang expansion. Crown Prince Naka no Ōe, later to become Emperor Tenji, and Empress Saimei decided to dispatch an expeditionary force to restore the Baekje kingdom, but this was decisively defeated by the Silla-Tang alliance at the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|en|Battle of Baekgang|WD=Q608727|lt=Battle of Baekgang}} in 663 AD. This ended Japanese involvement in the Korean Peninsula for centuries, and fearing that Japan would be subject to a counter-invasion by Silla-Tang armies, a large number of mountain-top castles were constructed across western Japan. It was with this background that Emperor Tenchi decided to relocate the capital from {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka}} to the shore of {{ill|Lake Biwa|en|Lake Biwa|WD=Q200239|lt=Lake Biwa}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province}}. The reason for the transfer is not entirely clear. It is speculated that the location of the new capital on the shores of Lake Biwa facilitated communications and transportation to the {{ill|Sea of Japan|en|Sea of Japan|WD=Q27092|lt=Sea of Japan}} coast, and the location was on the major ancient {{ill|Tōsandō|en|Tōsandō|WD=Q1334011|lt=Tōsandō}} and {{ill|Hokurikudō|en|Hokurikudō|WD=Q3139239|lt=Hokurikudō}} highways. Another possible reason was that the emperor felt the need to relocate from the entrenched political factions and interests in Asuka in order to have a freer hand at the radical reforms necessary to create a new political system toEICcounter the threats from abroad. In any case, per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', there was great dissatisfaction in Asuka regarding the move, which led to major riots and arson.
Emperor Tenchi died in 671. Emperor Tenchi had originally designated his brother, [[:en:Prince Ōama|Prince Ōama]], as his successor, but later changed his mind in favor of his son, {{ill|Emperor Kōbun|en|Emperor Kōbun|WD=Q357242|lt=Prince Ōtomo}}. This led to the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War}}, during which Prince Ōtomo killed himself after reigning for less than a year, and the Ōtsu Palace was burned down. Prince Ōama succeeded to the throne as the {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|WD=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu}}, and moved the capital back to Asuka.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
In the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, the capital was moved to [[Heijō-kyō]], and the {{ill|Ōmi Kokuchō|en|Ōmi Kokuchō|WD=Q11638479|lt=provincial capital of Ōmi Province}} was built at Seta (now a neighborhood of the city of Ōtsu). The site of ruins of the Ōtsu Palace was named {{nihongo|Furutsu|古津||}}, meaning "old port", but the name was restored to "Ōtsu" by order of [[Emperor Kanmu]]. However, over time the exact location of the palace became lost in later centuries, and especially with growing nationalism in the late {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} and {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō era}}, there was strong desire to relocate it. Theories were advanced whenever ruins were found in the approximate location, which included the sites of {{ill|Sūfuku-ji (Ōtsu)|en|Sūfuku-ji (Ōtsu)|WD=Q11477101|lt=Sūfuku-ji}} and the {{ill|Minamishigachō temple ruins|en|Minamishigachō temple ruins|WD=Q109239206|lt=Minamishigachō temple ruins}} amongst many other possible candidates. As the palace existed for only five years and did not make use of [[:en:Roof tile|Roof tile]]s, very little physical evidence survived. In 1974, during {{ill|Rescue archaeology|en|Rescue archaeology|WD=Q653838|lt=Rescue archaeology}} on the site of a new residential area in the Nishikiori neighborhood of Ōtsu, 13 pillar holes which are thought to have been the remnants of a monumental south-facing gate were discovered. Further [[archaeological excavation]]s have found {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue}} and {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}} pottery, the presence of a double corridor extending from the South Gate to a large main hall, and the remnants of moats and earthen ramparts. The layout of structures were similar to that of the {{ill|Naniwa-kyō|en|Naniwa-kyō|WD=Q1042338|lt=Naniwa Palace}} which had been discovered in Osaka and which was nearly contemporary with the Ōtsu Palace. The total area of the Ōtsu Palace site is estimated to be 700 meters north-to-south by 400 meters east-to-west.EICThe site has been preserved as an [[:en:Archaeological park|Archaeological park]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The ruins of the Ōmi Ōtsu Palace are located in the western part of modern city of Ōtsu, about 500 meters north of {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company}} [[:en:Nishi-Ōtsu Station|Nishi-Ōtsu Station]] or a two-minute walk from {{ill|Ōmijingūmae Station|en|Ōmijingūmae Station|WD=Q8081616|lt=Ōmijingūmae Station}} on the {{ill|Keihan Electric Railway|en|Keihan Electric Railway|WD=Q1188274|lt=Keihan Electric Railway}} [[:en:Ishiyama Sakamoto Line|Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shiga)]]
==References== *{{cite book | last = Whitney Hall | first = John |author2=Delmer M Brown |author3=Kozo Yamamura | title = The Cambridge History of Japan | date = 1988 | pages = 42–44 | publisher = Cambridge University Press | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=nCJwEDzyxNgC&dq=Shigaraki+miya&pg=PA42 | isbn = 0-521-22352-0 }}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace}} *[https://www.biwako-visitors.jp/spot/detail/971/Shiga-Biwako Tourist Information] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.rekihaku.otsu.shiga.jp/event/jyousetsu/theme_6.html Otsu City Museum of History] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://oumijingu.org/pages/112/ Omi Jingu homepage] {{in lang|ja}}

It is speculated that the location of the new capital on the shores of Lake Biwa facilitated communications and transportation to the {{ill|Sea of Japan|en|Sea of Japan|WD=Q27092|lt=Sea of Japan}} coast, and the location was on the major ancient {{ill|Tōsandō|en|Tōsandō|WD=Q1334011|lt=Tōsandō}} and {{ill|Hokurikudō|en|Hokurikudō|WD=Q3139239|lt=Hokurikudō}} highways. Another possible reason was that the emperor felt the need to relocate from the entrenched political factions and interests in Asuka in order to have a freer hand at the radical reforms necessary to create a new political system toEICcounter the threats from abroad. In any case, per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', there was great dissatisfaction in Asuka regarding the move, which led to major riots and arson.
Emperor Tenchi died in 671. Emperor Tenchi had originally designated his brother, [[:en:Prince Ōama|Prince Ōama]], as his successor, but later changed his mind in favor of his son, {{ill|Emperor Kōbun|en|Emperor Kōbun|WD=Q357242|lt=Prince Ōtomo}}. This led to the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War}}, during which Prince Ōtomo killed himself after reigning for less than a year, and the Ōtsu Palace was burned down. Prince Ōama succeeded to the throne as the {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|WD=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu}}, and moved the capital back to Asuka.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
In the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, the capital was moved to [[Heijō-kyō]], and the {{ill|Ōmi Kokuchō|en|Ōmi Kokuchō|WD=Q11638479|lt=provincial capital of Ōmi Province}} was built at Seta (now a neighborhood of the city of Ōtsu). The site of ruins of the Ōtsu Palace was named {{nihongo|Furutsu|古津||}}, meaning "old port", but the name was restored to "Ōtsu" by order of [[Emperor Kanmu]]. However, over time the exact location of the palace became lost in later centuries, and especially with growing nationalism in the late {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} and {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō era}}, there was strong desire to relocate it. Theories were advanced whenever ruins were found in the approximate location, which included the sites of {{ill|Sūfuku-ji (Ōtsu)|en|Sūfuku-ji (Ōtsu)|WD=Q11477101|lt=Sūfuku-ji}} and the {{ill|Minamishigachō temple ruins|en|Minamishigachō temple ruins|WD=Q109239206|lt=Minamishigachō temple ruins}} amongst many other possible candidates. As the palace existed for only five years and did not make use of [[:en:Roof tile|Roof tile]]s, very little physical evidence survived. In 1974, during {{ill|Rescue archaeology|en|Rescue archaeology|WD=Q653838|lt=Rescue archaeology}} on the site of a new residential area in the Nishikiori neighborhood of Ōtsu, 13 pillar holes which are thought to have been the remnants of a monumental south-facing gate were discovered. Further [[archaeological excavation]]s have found {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue}} and {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}} pottery, the presence of a double corridor extending from the South Gate to a large main hall, and the remnants of moats and earthen ramparts. The layout of structures were similar to that of the {{ill|Naniwa-kyō|en|Naniwa-kyō|WD=Q1042338|lt=Naniwa Palace}} which had been discovered in Osaka and which was nearly contemporary with the Ōtsu Palace. The total area of the Ōtsu Palace site is estimated to be 700 meters north-to-south by 400 meters east-to-west.EICThe site has been preserved as an [[:en:Archaeological park|Archaeological park]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The ruins of the Ōmi Ōtsu Palace are located in the western part of modern city of Ōtsu, about 500 meters north of {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company}} [[:en:Nishi-Ōtsu Station|Nishi-Ōtsu Station]] or a two-minute walk from {{ill|Ōmijingūmae Station|en|Ōmijingūmae Station|WD=Q8081616|lt=Ōmijingūmae Station}} on the {{ill|Keihan Electric Railway|en|Keihan Electric Railway|WD=Q1188274|lt=Keihan Electric Railway}} [[:en:Ishiyama Sakamoto Line|Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shiga)]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]][[en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]][[fa:قصر اومی اوتسو]][[ja:近江大津宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]][[en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]][[fa:قصر اومی اوتسو]][[ja:近江大津宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]][[en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]][[fa:قصر اومی اوتسو]][[ja:近江大津宮]]
{{Infobox ancient site | name =Ōmi Ōtsu Palace | native_name =近江大津宮 | native_name_lang = | image = Omi Otsu-no-miya, dairi-seiden-ato.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Ōmi Ōtsu Palace ruins | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ōmi Ōtsu Palace | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|1|41.9|N|135|51|18.0|E|region:JP-25|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu, Shiga]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} | type = palace ruins | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 7th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}} | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no public facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Ōmi Ōtsu Palace'''|近江大津宮| Ōmi Ōtsu-no-miya}} was an imperial palace built by [[:en:Emperor Tenchi|Emperor Tenchi]] in {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}} Japan in what is now the city of {{ill|Ōtsu|en|Ōtsu|WD=Q202907|lt=Ōtsu}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture}}, Japan. It served as the capital of Japan for a five-year period from 667 to 672 AD. Also known as the {{nihongo|'''Ōtsu Palace'''|大津宮| Ōtsu-no-miya}}, {{nihongo|'''Shiga no Miyako'''|志賀の都||}}, it was most frequently referred to in ancient sources as the {{nihongo|'''Ōmi Ōtsu-no-miya'''|水海大津宮||}}.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> It was at this location that the {{ill|Ōmi Code|en|Ōmi Code|WD=Q526944|lt=Ōmi Code}} and the family registry system were promulgated, which laid the foundations for the later ''[[ritsuryō]]'' state. It location was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1979, with the area under protection expanded in 2007<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/163041|title=近江大津宮錦織遺跡|trans-title= Ōmi Ōtsu PalaceNishigori Site|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref>
==History== In 660 AD, during the reign of [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]], the Korean kingdom of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] fell to an alliance of {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China}}. Baekje was a close ally of ancient Japan, and viewed as a bulwark against possible Silla or Tang expansion. Crown Prince Naka no Ōe, later to become Emperor Tenji, and Empress Saimei decided to dispatch an expeditionary force to restore the Baekje kingdom, but this was decisively defeated by the Silla-Tang alliance at the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|en|Battle of Baekgang|WD=Q608727|lt=Battle of Baekgang}} in 663 AD. This ended Japanese involvement in the Korean Peninsula for centuries, and fearing that Japan would be subject to a counter-invasion by Silla-Tang armies, a large number of mountain-top castles were constructed across western Japan. It was with this background that Emperor Tenchi decided to relocate the capital from {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka}} to the shore of {{ill|Lake Biwa|en|Lake Biwa|WD=Q200239|lt=Lake Biwa}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province}}. The reason for the transfer is not entirely clear. It is speculated that the location of the new capital on the shores of Lake Biwa facilitated communications and transportation to the {{ill|Sea of Japan|en|Sea of Japan|WD=Q27092|lt=Sea of Japan}} coast, and the location was on the major ancient {{ill|Tōsandō|en|Tōsandō|WD=Q1334011|lt=Tōsandō}} and {{ill|Hokurikudō|en|Hokurikudō|WD=Q3139239|lt=Hokurikudō}} highways. Another possible reason was that the emperor felt the need to relocate from the entrenched political factions and interests in Asuka in order to have a freer hand at the radical reforms necessary to create a new political system toEICcounter the threats from abroad. In any case, per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', there was great dissatisfaction in Asuka regarding the move, which led to major riots and arson.
Emperor Tenchi died in 671. Emperor Tenchi had originally designated his brother, [[:en:Prince Ōama|Prince Ōama]], as his successor, but later changed his mind in favor of his son, {{ill|Emperor Kōbun|en|Emperor Kōbun|WD=Q357242|lt=Prince Ōtomo}}. This led to the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War}}, during which Prince Ōtomo killed himself after reigning for less than a year, and the Ōtsu Palace was burned down. Prince Ōama succeeded to the throne as the {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|WD=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu}}, and moved the capital back to Asuka.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
In the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, the capital was moved to [[Heijō-kyō]], and the {{ill|Ōmi Kokuchō|en|Ōmi Kokuchō|WD=Q11638479|lt=provincial capital of Ōmi Province}} was built at Seta (now a neighborhood of the city of Ōtsu). The site of ruins of the Ōtsu Palace was named {{nihongo|Furutsu|古津||}}, meaning "old port", but the name was restored to "Ōtsu" by order of [[Emperor Kanmu]]. However, over time the exact location of the palace became lost in later centuries, and especially with growing nationalism in the late {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} and {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō era}}, there was strong desire to relocate it. Theories were advanced whenever ruins were found in the approximate location, which included the sites of {{ill|Sūfuku-ji (Ōtsu)|en|Sūfuku-ji (Ōtsu)|WD=Q11477101|lt=Sūfuku-ji}} and the {{ill|Minamishigachō temple ruins|en|Minamishigachō temple ruins|WD=Q109239206|lt=Minamishigachō temple ruins}} amongst many other possible candidates. As the palace existed for only five years and did not make use of [[:en:Roof tile|Roof tile]]s, very little physical evidence survived. In 1974, during {{ill|Rescue archaeology|en|Rescue archaeology|WD=Q653838|lt=Rescue archaeology}} on the site of a new residential area in the Nishikiori neighborhood of Ōtsu, 13 pillar holes which are thought to have been the remnants of a monumental south-facing gate were discovered. Further [[archaeological excavation]]s have found {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue}} and {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}} pottery, the presence of a double corridor extending from the South Gate to a large main hall, and the remnants of moats and earthen ramparts. The layout of structures were similar to that of the {{ill|Naniwa-kyō|en|Naniwa-kyō|WD=Q1042338|lt=Naniwa Palace}} which had been discovered in Osaka and which was nearly contemporary with the Ōtsu Palace. The total area of the Ōtsu Palace site is estimated to be 700 meters north-to-south by 400 meters east-to-west.EICThe site has been preserved as an [[:en:Archaeological park|Archaeological park]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The ruins of the Ōmi Ōtsu Palace are located in the western part of modern city of Ōtsu, about 500 meters north of {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company}} [[:en:Nishi-Ōtsu Station|Nishi-Ōtsu Station]] or a two-minute walk from {{ill|Ōmijingūmae Station|en|Ōmijingūmae Station|WD=Q8081616|lt=Ōmijingūmae Station}} on the {{ill|Keihan Electric Railway|en|Keihan Electric Railway|WD=Q1188274|lt=Keihan Electric Railway}} [[:en:Ishiyama Sakamoto Line|Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The layout of structures were similar to that of the {{ill|Naniwa-kyō|en|Naniwa-kyō|WD=Q1042338|lt=Naniwa Palace}} which had been discovered in Osaka and which was nearly contemporary with the Ōtsu Palace. The total area of the Ōtsu Palace site is estimated to be 700 meters north-to-south by 400 meters east-to-west.EICThe site has been preserved as an [[:en:Archaeological park|Archaeological park]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The ruins of the Ōmi Ōtsu Palace are located in the western part of modern city of Ōtsu, about 500 meters north of {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company}} [[:en:Nishi-Ōtsu Station|Nishi-Ōtsu Station]] or a two-minute walk from {{ill|Ōmijingūmae Station|en|Ōmijingūmae Station|WD=Q8081616|lt=Ōmijingūmae Station}} on the {{ill|Keihan Electric Railway|en|Keihan Electric Railway|WD=Q1188274|lt=Keihan Electric Railway}} [[:en:Ishiyama Sakamoto Line|Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shiga)]]
==References== *{{cite book | last = Whitney Hall | first = John |author2=Delmer M Brown |author3=Kozo Yamamura | title = The Cambridge History of Japan | date = 1988 | pages = 42–44 | publisher = Cambridge University Press | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=nCJwEDzyxNgC&dq=Shigaraki+miya&pg=PA42 | isbn = 0-521-22352-0 }}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace}} *[https://www.biwako-visitors.jp/spot/detail/971/Shiga-Biwako Tourist Information] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.rekihaku.otsu.shiga.jp/event/jyousetsu/theme_6.html Otsu City Museum of History] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://oumijingu.org/pages/112/ Omi Jingu homepage] {{in lang|ja}}

revision.comment: Bot: fix ill template destination links
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]][[en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]][[fa:قصر اومی اوتسو]][[ja:近江大津宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]][[en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]][[fa:قصر اومی اوتسو]][[ja:近江大津宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]][[en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]][[fa:قصر اومی اوتسو]][[ja:近江大津宮]]
{{Infobox ancient site | name =Ōmi Ōtsu Palace | native_name =近江大津宮 | native_name_lang = | image = Omi Otsu-no-miya, dairi-seiden-ato.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Ōmi Ōtsu Palace ruins | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ōmi Ōtsu Palace | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|1|41.9|N|135|51|18.0|E|region:JP-25|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu, Shiga]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} | type = palace ruins | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 7th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no public facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Ōmi Ōtsu Palace'''|近江大津宮| Ōmi Ōtsu-no-miya}} was an imperial palace built by [[:en:Emperor Tenchi|Emperor Tenchi]] in {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} Japan in what is now the city of {{ill|Ōtsu|en|Ōtsu|WD=Q202907|lt=Ōtsu|1=Ōtsu}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture|1=Shiga Prefecture}}, Japan. It served as the capital of Japan for a five-year period from 667 to 672 AD. Also known as the {{nihongo|'''Ōtsu Palace'''|大津宮| Ōtsu-no-miya}}, {{nihongo|'''Shiga no Miyako'''|志賀の都||}}, it was most frequently referred to in ancient sources as the {{nihongo|'''Ōmi Ōtsu-no-miya'''|水海大津宮||}}.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> It was at this location that the {{ill|Ōmi Code|en|Ōmi Code|WD=Q526944|lt=Ōmi Code|1=Ōmi Code}} and the family registry system were promulgated, which laid the foundations for the later ''[[ritsuryō]]'' state. It location was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1979, with the area under protection expanded in 2007<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/163041|title=近江大津宮錦織遺跡|trans-title= Ōmi Ōtsu PalaceNishigori Site|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref>
==History== In 660 AD, during the reign of [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]], the Korean kingdom of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] fell to an alliance of {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China|1=Tang dynasty}}. Baekje was a close ally of ancient Japan, and viewed as a bulwark against possible Silla or Tang expansion. Crown Prince Naka no Ōe, later to become Emperor Tenji, and Empress Saimei decided to dispatch an expeditionary force to restore the Baekje kingdom, but this was decisively defeated by the Silla-Tang alliance at the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|en|Battle of Baekgang|WD=Q608727|lt=Battle of Baekgang|1=Battle of Baekgang}} in 663 AD. This ended Japanese involvement in the Korean Peninsula for centuries, and fearing that Japan would be subject to a counter-invasion by Silla-Tang armies, a large number of mountain-top castles were constructed across western Japan. It was with this background that Emperor Tenchi decided to relocate the capital from {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka|1=Asuka, Nara}} to the shore of {{ill|Lake Biwa|en|Lake Biwa|WD=Q200239|lt=Lake Biwa|1=Lake Biwa}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}}. The reason for the transfer is not entirely clear. It is speculated that the location of the new capital on the shores of Lake Biwa facilitated communications and transportation to the {{ill|Sea of Japan|en|Sea of Japan|WD=Q27092|lt=Sea of Japan|1=Sea of Japan}} coast, and the location was on the major ancient {{ill|Tōsandō|en|Tōsandō|WD=Q1334011|lt=Tōsandō|1=Tōsandō}} and {{ill|Hokurikudō|en|Hokurikudō|WD=Q3139239|lt=Hokurikudō|1=Hokurikudō}} highways. Another possible reason was that the emperor felt the need to relocate from the entrenched political factions and interests in Asuka in order to have a freer hand at the radical reforms necessary to create a new political system toEICcounter the threats from abroad. In any case, per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', there was great dissatisfaction in Asuka regarding the move, which led to major riots and arson.
Emperor Tenchi died in 671. Emperor Tenchi had originally designated his brother, [[:en:Prince Ōama|Prince Ōama]], as his successor, but later changed his mind in favor of his son, {{ill|Emperor Kōbun|en|Emperor Kōbun|WD=Q357242|lt=Prince Ōtomo|1=Emperor Kōbun}}. This led to the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War|1=Jinshin War}}, during which Prince Ōtomo killed himself after reigning for less than a year, and the Ōtsu Palace was burned down. Prince Ōama succeeded to the throne as the {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|WD=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}, and moved the capital back to Asuka.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
In the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, the capital was moved to [[Heijō-kyō]], and the {{ill|Ōmi Kokuchō|en|Ōmi Kokuchō|WD=Q11638479|lt=provincial capital of Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Kokuchō}} was built at Seta (now a neighborhood of the city of Ōtsu). The site of ruins of the Ōtsu Palace was named {{nihongo|Furutsu|古津||}}, meaning "old port", but the name was restored to "Ōtsu" by order of [[Emperor Kanmu]]. However, over time the exact location of the palace became lost in later centuries, and especially with growing nationalism in the late {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} and {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō era|1=Taishō era}}, there was strong desire to relocate it. Theories were advanced whenever ruins were found in the approximate location, which included the sites of {{ill|Sūfuku-ji (Ōtsu)|en|Sūfuku-ji (Ōtsu)|WD=Q11477101|lt=Sūfuku-ji|1=Sūfuku-ji (Ōtsu)}} and the {{ill|Minamishigachō temple ruins|en|Minamishigachō temple ruins|WD=Q109239206|lt=Minamishigachō temple ruins|1=Minamishigachō temple ruins}} amongst many other possible candidates. As the palace existed for only five years and did not make use of [[:en:Roof tile|Roof tile]]s, very little physical evidence survived. In 1974, during {{ill|Rescue archaeology|en|Rescue archaeology|WD=Q653838|lt=Rescue archaeology|1=Rescue archaeology}} on the site of a new residential area in the Nishikiori neighborhood of Ōtsu, 13 pillar holes which are thought to have been the remnants of a monumental south-facing gate were discovered. Further [[archaeological excavation]]s have found {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue|1=Sue pottery}} and {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}} pottery, the presence of a double corridor extending from the South Gate to a large main hall, and the remnants of moats and earthen ramparts. The layout of structures were similar to that of the {{ill|Naniwa-kyō|en|Naniwa-kyō|WD=Q1042338|lt=Naniwa Palace|1=Naniwa-kyō}} which had been discovered in Osaka and which was nearly contemporary with the Ōtsu Palace. The total area of the Ōtsu Palace site is estimated to be 700 meters north-to-south by 400 meters east-to-west.EICThe site has been preserved as an [[:en:Archaeological park|Archaeological park]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The ruins of the Ōmi Ōtsu Palace are located in the western part of modern city of Ōtsu, about 500 meters north of {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} [[:en:Nishi-Ōtsu Station|Nishi-Ōtsu Station]] or a two-minute walk from {{ill|Ōmijingūmae Station|en|Ōmijingūmae Station|WD=Q8081616|lt=Ōmijingūmae Station|1=Ōmijingūmae Station}} on the {{ill|Keihan Electric Railway|en|Keihan Electric Railway|WD=Q1188274|lt=Keihan Electric Railway|1=Keihan Electric Railway}} [[:en:Ishiyama Sakamoto Line|Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shiga)]]
==References== *{{cite book | last = Whitney Hall | first = John |author2=Delmer M Brown |author3=Kozo Yamamura | title = The Cambridge History of Japan | date = 1988 | pages = 42–44 | publisher = Cambridge University Press | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=nCJwEDzyxNgC&dq=Shigaraki+miya&pg=PA42 | isbn = 0-521-22352-0 }}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace}} *[https://www.biwako-visitors.jp/spot/detail/971/Shiga-Biwako Tourist Information] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.rekihaku.otsu.shiga.jp/event/jyousetsu/theme_6.html Otsu City Museum of History] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://oumijingu.org/pages/112/ Omi Jingu homepage] {{in lang|ja}}
==Notes== {{reflist}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]][[en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]][[fa:قصر اومی اوتسو]][[ja:近江大津宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]][[en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]][[fa:قصر اومی اوتسو]][[ja:近江大津宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]][[en:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace]][[fa:قصر اومی اوتسو]][[ja:近江大津宮]]
{{Infobox ancient site | name =Ōmi Ōtsu Palace | native_name =近江大津宮 | native_name_lang = | image = Omi Otsu-no-miya, dairi-seiden-ato.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Ōmi Ōtsu Palace ruins | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ōmi Ōtsu Palace | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|1|41.9|N|135|51|18.0|E|region:JP-25|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu, Shiga]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} | type = palace ruins | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 7th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no public facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Ōmi Ōtsu Palace'''|近江大津宮| Ōmi Ōtsu-no-miya}} was an imperial palace built by [[:en:Emperor Tenchi|Emperor Tenchi]] in {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} Japan in what is now the city of {{ill|Ōtsu|en|Ōtsu|WD=Q202907|lt=Ōtsu|1=Ōtsu}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture|1=Shiga Prefecture}}, Japan. It served as the capital of Japan for a five-year period from 667 to 672 AD. Also known as the {{nihongo|'''Ōtsu Palace'''|大津宮| Ōtsu-no-miya}}, {{nihongo|'''Shiga no Miyako'''|志賀の都||}}, it was most frequently referred to in ancient sources as the {{nihongo|'''Ōmi Ōtsu-no-miya'''|水海大津宮||}}.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> It was at this location that the {{ill|Ōmi Code|en|Ōmi Code|WD=Q526944|lt=Ōmi Code|1=Ōmi Code}} and the family registry system were promulgated, which laid the foundations for the later ''[[ritsuryō]]'' state. It location was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1979, with the area under protection expanded in 2007<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/163041|title=近江大津宮錦織遺跡|trans-title= Ōmi Ōtsu PalaceNishigori Site|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref>
==History== In 660 AD, during the reign of [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]], the Korean kingdom of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] fell to an alliance of {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China|1=Tang dynasty}}. Baekje was a close ally of ancient Japan, and viewed as a bulwark against possible Silla or Tang expansion. Crown Prince Naka no Ōe, later to become Emperor Tenji, and Empress Saimei decided to dispatch an expeditionary force to restore the Baekje kingdom, but this was decisively defeated by the Silla-Tang alliance at the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|en|Battle of Baekgang|WD=Q608727|lt=Battle of Baekgang|1=Battle of Baekgang}} in 663 AD. This ended Japanese involvement in the Korean Peninsula for centuries, and fearing that Japan would be subject to a counter-invasion by Silla-Tang armies, a large number of mountain-top castles were constructed across western Japan. It was with this background that Emperor Tenchi decided to relocate the capital from {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka|1=Asuka, Nara}} to the shore of {{ill|Lake Biwa|en|Lake Biwa|WD=Q200239|lt=Lake Biwa|1=Lake Biwa}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}}. The reason for the transfer is not entirely clear. It is speculated that the location of the new capital on the shores of Lake Biwa facilitated communications and transportation to the {{ill|Sea of Japan|en|Sea of Japan|WD=Q27092|lt=Sea of Japan|1=Sea of Japan}} coast, and the location was on the major ancient {{ill|Tōsandō|en|Tōsandō|WD=Q1334011|lt=Tōsandō|1=Tōsandō}} and {{ill|Hokurikudō|en|Hokurikudō|WD=Q3139239|lt=Hokurikudō|1=Hokurikudō}} highways. Another possible reason was that the emperor felt the need to relocate from the entrenched political factions and interests in Asuka in order to have a freer hand at the radical reforms necessary to create a new political system toEICcounter the threats from abroad. In any case, per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', there was great dissatisfaction in Asuka regarding the move, which led to major riots and arson.
Emperor Tenchi died in 671. Emperor Tenchi had originally designated his brother, [[:en:Prince Ōama|Prince Ōama]], as his successor, but later changed his mind in favor of his son, {{ill|Emperor Kōbun|en|Emperor Kōbun|WD=Q357242|lt=Prince Ōtomo|1=Emperor Kōbun}}. This led to the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War|1=Jinshin War}}, during which Prince Ōtomo killed himself after reigning for less than a year, and the Ōtsu Palace was burned down. Prince Ōama succeeded to the throne as the {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|WD=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}, and moved the capital back to Asuka.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
In the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, the capital was moved to [[Heijō-kyō]], and the {{ill|Ōmi Kokuchō|en|Ōmi Kokuchō|WD=Q11638479|lt=provincial capital of Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Kokuchō}} was built at Seta (now a neighborhood of the city of Ōtsu). The site of ruins of the Ōtsu Palace was named {{nihongo|Furutsu|古津||}}, meaning "old port", but the name was restored to "Ōtsu" by order of [[Emperor Kanmu]]. However, over time the exact location of the palace became lost in later centuries, and especially with growing nationalism in the late {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} and {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō era|1=Taishō era}}, there was strong desire to relocate it. Theories were advanced whenever ruins were found in the approximate location, which included the sites of {{ill|Sūfuku-ji (Ōtsu)|en|Sūfuku-ji (Ōtsu)|WD=Q11477101|lt=Sūfuku-ji|1=Sūfuku-ji (Ōtsu)}} and the {{ill|Minamishigachō temple ruins|en|Minamishigachō temple ruins|WD=Q109239206|lt=Minamishigachō temple ruins|1=Minamishigachō temple ruins}} amongst many other possible candidates. As the palace existed for only five years and did not make use of [[:en:Roof tile|Roof tile]]s, very little physical evidence survived. In 1974, during {{ill|Rescue archaeology|en|Rescue archaeology|WD=Q653838|lt=Rescue archaeology|1=Rescue archaeology}} on the site of a new residential area in the Nishikiori neighborhood of Ōtsu, 13 pillar holes which are thought to have been the remnants of a monumental south-facing gate were discovered. Further [[archaeological excavation]]s have found {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue|1=Sue pottery}} and {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}} pottery, the presence of a double corridor extending from the South Gate to a large main hall, and the remnants of moats and earthen ramparts. The layout of structures were similar to that of the {{ill|Naniwa-kyō|en|Naniwa-kyō|WD=Q1042338|lt=Naniwa Palace|1=Naniwa-kyō}} which had been discovered in Osaka and which was nearly contemporary with the Ōtsu Palace. The total area of the Ōtsu Palace site is estimated to be 700 meters north-to-south by 400 meters east-to-west.EICThe site has been preserved as an [[:en:Archaeological park|Archaeological park]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The ruins of the Ōmi Ōtsu Palace are located in the western part of modern city of Ōtsu, about 500 meters north of {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} [[:en:Nishi-Ōtsu Station|Nishi-Ōtsu Station]] or a two-minute walk from {{ill|Ōmijingūmae Station|en|Ōmijingūmae Station|WD=Q8081616|lt=Ōmijingūmae Station|1=Ōmijingūmae Station}} on the {{ill|Keihan Electric Railway|en|Keihan Electric Railway|WD=Q1188274|lt=Keihan Electric Railway|1=Keihan Electric Railway}} [[:en:Ishiyama Sakamoto Line|Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shiga)]]
==References== *{{cite book | last = Whitney Hall | first = John |author2=Delmer M Brown |author3=Kozo Yamamura | title = The Cambridge History of Japan | date = 1988 | pages = 42–44 | publisher = Cambridge University Press | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=nCJwEDzyxNgC&dq=Shigaraki+miya&pg=PA42 | isbn = 0-521-22352-0 }}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace}} *[https://www.biwako-visitors.jp/spot/detail/971/Shiga-Biwako Tourist Information] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.rekihaku.otsu.shiga.jp/event/jyousetsu/theme_6.html Otsu City Museum of History] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://oumijingu.org/pages/112/ Omi Jingu homepage] {{in lang|ja}}

{{Infobox ancient site | name =Ōmi Ōtsu Palace | native_name =近江大津宮 | native_name_lang = | image = Omi Otsu-no-miya, dairi-seiden-ato.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Ōmi Ōtsu Palace ruins | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ōmi Ōtsu Palace | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|1|41.9|N|135|51|18.0|E|region:JP-25|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu, Shiga]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} | type = palace ruins | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 7th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no public facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Ōmi Ōtsu Palace'''|近江大津宮| Ōmi Ōtsu-no-miya}} was an imperial palace built by [[:en:Emperor Tenchi|Emperor Tenchi]] in {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} Japan in what is now the city of {{ill|Ōtsu|en|Ōtsu|WD=Q202907|lt=Ōtsu|1=Ōtsu}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture|1=Shiga Prefecture}}, Japan. It served as the capital of Japan for a five-year period from 667 to 672 AD. Also known as the {{nihongo|'''Ōtsu Palace'''|大津宮| Ōtsu-no-miya}}, {{nihongo|'''Shiga no Miyako'''|志賀の都||}}, it was most frequently referred to in ancient sources as the {{nihongo|'''Ōmi Ōtsu-no-miya'''|水海大津宮||}}.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> It was at this location that the {{ill|Ōmi Code|en|Ōmi Code|WD=Q526944|lt=Ōmi Code|1=Ōmi Code}} and the family registry system were promulgated, which laid the foundations for the later ''[[ritsuryō]]'' state. It location was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1979, with the area under protection expanded in 2007<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/163041|title=近江大津宮錦織遺跡|trans-title= Ōmi Ōtsu PalaceNishigori Site|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref>
==History== In 660 AD, during the reign of [[:en:Empress Saimei|Empress Saimei]], the Korean kingdom of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] fell to an alliance of {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China|1=Tang dynasty}}. Baekje was a close ally of ancient Japan, and viewed as a bulwark against possible Silla or Tang expansion. Crown Prince Naka no Ōe, later to become Emperor Tenji, and Empress Saimei decided to dispatch an expeditionary force to restore the Baekje kingdom, but this was decisively defeated by the Silla-Tang alliance at the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|en|Battle of Baekgang|WD=Q608727|lt=Battle of Baekgang|1=Battle of Baekgang}} in 663 AD. This ended Japanese involvement in the Korean Peninsula for centuries, and fearing that Japan would be subject to a counter-invasion by Silla-Tang armies, a large number of mountain-top castles were constructed across western Japan. It was with this background that Emperor Tenchi decided to relocate the capital from {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka|1=Asuka, Nara}} to the shore of {{ill|Lake Biwa|en|Lake Biwa|WD=Q200239|lt=Lake Biwa|1=Lake Biwa}} in {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}}. The reason for the transfer is not entirely clear. It is speculated that the location of the new capital on the shores of Lake Biwa facilitated communications and transportation to the {{ill|Sea of Japan|en|Sea of Japan|WD=Q27092|lt=Sea of Japan|1=Sea of Japan}} coast, and the location was on the major ancient {{ill|Tōsandō|en|Tōsandō|WD=Q1334011|lt=Tōsandō|1=Tōsandō}} and {{ill|Hokurikudō|en|Hokurikudō|WD=Q3139239|lt=Hokurikudō|1=Hokurikudō}} highways. Another possible reason was that the emperor felt the need to relocate from the entrenched political factions and interests in Asuka in order to have a freer hand at the radical reforms necessary to create a new political system toEICcounter the threats from abroad. In any case, per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', there was great dissatisfaction in Asuka regarding the move, which led to major riots and arson.
Emperor Tenchi died in 671. Emperor Tenchi had originally designated his brother, [[:en:Prince Ōama|Prince Ōama]], as his successor, but later changed his mind in favor of his son, {{ill|Emperor Kōbun|en|Emperor Kōbun|WD=Q357242|lt=Prince Ōtomo|1=Emperor Kōbun}}. This led to the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War|1=Jinshin War}}, during which Prince Ōtomo killed himself after reigning for less than a year, and the Ōtsu Palace was burned down. Prince Ōama succeeded to the throne as the {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|WD=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}, and moved the capital back to Asuka.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
In the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, the capital was moved to [[Heijō-kyō]], and the {{ill|Ōmi Kokuchō|en|Ōmi Kokuchō|WD=Q11638479|lt=provincial capital of Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Kokuchō}} was built at Seta (now a neighborhood of the city of Ōtsu). The site of ruins of the Ōtsu Palace was named {{nihongo|Furutsu|古津||}}, meaning "old port", but the name was restored to "Ōtsu" by order of [[Emperor Kanmu]]. However, over time the exact location of the palace became lost in later centuries, and especially with growing nationalism in the late {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} and {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō era|1=Taishō era}}, there was strong desire to relocate it. Theories were advanced whenever ruins were found in the approximate location, which included the sites of {{ill|Sūfuku-ji (Ōtsu)|en|Sūfuku-ji (Ōtsu)|WD=Q11477101|lt=Sūfuku-ji|1=Sūfuku-ji (Ōtsu)}} and the {{ill|Minamishigachō temple ruins|en|Minamishigachō temple ruins|WD=Q109239206|lt=Minamishigachō temple ruins|1=Minamishigachō temple ruins}} amongst many other possible candidates. As the palace existed for only five years and did not make use of [[:en:Roof tile|Roof tile]]s, very little physical evidence survived. In 1974, during {{ill|Rescue archaeology|en|Rescue archaeology|WD=Q653838|lt=Rescue archaeology|1=Rescue archaeology}} on the site of a new residential area in the Nishikiori neighborhood of Ōtsu, 13 pillar holes which are thought to have been the remnants of a monumental south-facing gate were discovered. Further [[archaeological excavation]]s have found {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue|1=Sue pottery}} and {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}} pottery, the presence of a double corridor extending from the South Gate to a large main hall, and the remnants of moats and earthen ramparts. The layout of structures were similar to that of the {{ill|Naniwa-kyō|en|Naniwa-kyō|WD=Q1042338|lt=Naniwa Palace|1=Naniwa-kyō}} which had been discovered in Osaka and which was nearly contemporary with the Ōtsu Palace. The total area of the Ōtsu Palace site is estimated to be 700 meters north-to-south by 400 meters east-to-west.EICThe site has been preserved as an [[:en:Archaeological park|Archaeological park]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The ruins of the Ōmi Ōtsu Palace are located in the western part of modern city of Ōtsu, about 500 meters north of {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} [[:en:Nishi-Ōtsu Station|Nishi-Ōtsu Station]] or a two-minute walk from {{ill|Ōmijingūmae Station|en|Ōmijingūmae Station|WD=Q8081616|lt=Ōmijingūmae Station|1=Ōmijingūmae Station}} on the {{ill|Keihan Electric Railway|en|Keihan Electric Railway|WD=Q1188274|lt=Keihan Electric Railway|1=Keihan Electric Railway}} [[:en:Ishiyama Sakamoto Line|Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shiga)]]
==References== *{{cite book | last = Whitney Hall | first = John |author2=Delmer M Brown |author3=Kozo Yamamura | title = The Cambridge History of Japan | date = 1988 | pages = 42–44 | publisher = Cambridge University Press | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=nCJwEDzyxNgC&dq=Shigaraki+miya&pg=PA42 | isbn = 0-521-22352-0 }}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:Ōmi Ōtsu Palace}} *[https://www.biwako-visitors.jp/spot/detail/971/Shiga-Biwako Tourist Information] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.rekihaku.otsu.shiga.jp/event/jyousetsu/theme_6.html Otsu City Museum of History] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://oumijingu.org/pages/112/ Omi Jingu homepage] {{in lang|ja}}
